text: FAOUET (LE), a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Finistère, 28 m. W by S of Pontivy. Pop. of town in 1841, 2,662; of cant. 13,801. The town has manufactories of paper and potato-starch.

FAR, or FARR, a mountainous parish in Sutherlandshire, extending about 40 m. in length from NE to SW; with a breadth varying from 3 to 20 m. It is wholly the property of the duke of Sutherland; and is chiefly laid out in sheep-walks. The soil is in general barren and shallow; but on the banks of the rivers Naver and Borgie it is deep, and tolerably fertile. Strathnaver extends from the coast to the roots of Beinchlibrig, a distance of above 30 m. The extent of sea-coast is 13 m.; the shore is high and rocky, and consists of Strathy-head and bay, Armidale-bay, Far-head and bay, and several other smaller promontories and bays. The whole coast is excavated into extensive caves; which afford retreat to immense numbers of seals. Loch Naver is the principal lake in the district; and there are several smaller lakes from which rise a few rivulets. Beinchlibrig, the highest mountain in the district, [alt. 3,200 ft., is in the SW extremity of the parish. Pop. in 1801, 2,408; in 1831, 2,073; in 1841, 2,217.

FARA, a small town in the Papal states, in the deleg. of Riete, 15 m. N by W of Tivoli, and 22 m. NNW of Rome.—Also a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 11 m. NW of Novara. Pop. 1,804.—Also a town of Illyria, in the gov. of Trieste, on the r. bank of the Isonzo, 4 m. SW of Goritza. Pop. 900.
FARA, or Pharey, one of the Orkney islands, 2 m. W of Eday. It is 2 m. long, and 1 m. broad; and affords good pasture. Pop. 65.—Also one of the smaller Orkney islands, 1 m. E of Hoy.
FARA. See FARO.
FARA-FILIORUM-PETRI, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Citra, 6 m. SSE of Chieti, on the r. bank of the Foro. Pop. 1,500.
FARA-SAN-MARTINO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Citra, 11 m. SW of Lanciano, on the Aretino. Pop. 2,000.
FARADAY (Mount), a fine table-shaped mountain in Australia, nearly under the parallel of 25° S. Alt. 2,523 ft. It is apparently of volcanic formation.

FARADES, a village of Tunis, near the sea-coast, and 38 m. SE of Tunis. It was an old Roman town, probably the Veneria or Aphrodisium of the ancients. The inhabitants of F. in the 16th cent. were the most experienced mariners in the country; but Hamamet, which is immediately on the coast, has now in a great measure supplanted it.

FARAFREH, an oasis in the Libyan desert, in N lat. 27° 5', E long. 28° 24', 32 hours SSW of El- Hayz. It has one v. of about 180 inhabitants, who cultivate various little isolated spots of land around it, and to the E and SW.
FARAGLIONI, a group of prismatic amorphous rocks off the bay of La Trezza, on the E coast of Sicily, 11 m. NE of Aci-Castello. They were the Cyclopum Scopuli of the ancients.
FARAHABAD, a port of Persia, in the prov. of Mazanderan, on the coast of the Caspian, 15 m. N of Sari.
FARAHAN, a village of Persia, in the prov. of Irak-Ajemi, 75 m. SE of Hamadan.
FARAJGHAN, a village in Kafaristan, on the S declivity of the Hindu-kush, in N lat. 35° 42'.

FARALLONES, two clusters of rocks off the harbour of San Francisco in California, the southernmost of which lies exactly S 30° E true, 18 m. from Punta-de-los-Rayes. The smaller cluster lies to the NW. Beechey fixes the position of the Great F. in S lat. 37° 41′ 55", and 0° 35′ 18" W long. of San Francisco, and 122° 58' 28" W long. of Greenwich.
FARAMANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of la Cote-Saint-Andre. Pop. 1,593.
FARAN, a village of Senegambia, on the l. bank of the Rio-Grande, 45 m. SE of Kadé.
FARAN. See FEIRAN.
FARANAGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Dowlatabad, 36 m. S of Ahmednuggur.

FARANCU-MANBATA, a village of Central Africa, in the district of Ovasula, 4 m. SE of Fessadugu, near an affluent of the Joliba. It contains between 300 and 400 inhabitants. The surrounding country is generally level, and covered with red stones and gravel.—Caillie.

FARAS, a village of Lower Nubia, situated on the lofty bank of the W shore of the Nile, in the midst of straggling date-trees and sandy mounds, 20 m. below the second cataract. A little above the v. is a fine and fertile island in the river; and about half-an-hour to the SW are the ruins of a small temple.
FARASINA, one of the three channels by which the gulf of Quarnero communicates with the Adriatic. It lies between the island of Cherso and the main.
FARAT, a small river of Nubia, in the Bedjah, which falls into the Arabian gulf in lat. 21° 50' N.
FARBANNA, a town of Senegambia, on the Sanon-Kolez river, the cap. of Bambuk.
FARCETT, a chapelry in the p. of Standground, in Huntingdonshire, 3 m. SSE of Peterborough. Pop. 620.
FARCIENNES, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 4 m. E of Charleroi. Pop. 1,000. It has brick-works, collieries, and manufactures of lamp black.
FARCZAD, a village and district of Transylvania, in the Szekler country, 4 m. W of Udvarhely.
FARDAN, a town of Persia, in Sistan, 155 m. SSW of Zareng.
FARE, a mountain on the S skirts of the p. of Midmar, in Aberdeenshire. Alt. 1,793 ft.
FARE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. and 6 m. N of Berre. Pop. 813.

FAREHAM, a parish and sea-port of England, in the co. of Southampton, at the NW extremity of Portsmouth harbour. Area of p. 6,670 acres. Pop. 6,168.—The town, which has a station on the Southwestern railway, by which it is 41 m. NNW of Gosport, is a place of considerable trade. Cordage and sacking are manufactured here, also the coarser kinds of pottery; and a considerable trade is carried on in FAR coals. Sloops and smaller vessels are built at F.; but the prosperity of the place chiefly proceeds from its contiguity to the naval establishment at Portsmouth. During the summer-season this town is much resorted to for the purpose of sea-bathing. It is a polling-place for the S division of the co.
FAREINS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Saint-Trivier-sur-Moignans, on the I. bank of the Saone, 5 m. N of Trevoux. Pop. 1,188.
FARELLA, a small island in the E. Indian sea, in S lat. 0° 48', E long. 104° 27'.
FARELLONES. See FARALLONES.
FAREMOUTIERS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Rosoy-en-Brie, 3 m. W of Coulommiers. Pop. 1,018.
FAREWELL, a parish in Staffordshire, 2 m. NW of Lichfield. Area 470 acres. Pop. 203.

FAREWELL (CAPE), the S point of W. Greenland, on the N side of the entrance of Davis's straits, in N lat. 59° 49', W long. 43° 54' [Raper], N lat. 59° 38', W long. 42° 42' [Conn. de Tems]. Dr. West argues that Cape F., which was so named by Davies in 1585, is not, as is stated by Egede, Crantz, and Giesecke, on the island of Sermesok, but on another island considerably to the SE of it; and that Staten- Hoek is identical with it.—Also a cape on the NW coast of Tavai-Poenammoo, the N extremity of the middle island of New Zealand, S lat. 40° 31'.

FARFA, or Fabaris, a small river of the Pontifical states, which falls into the Tiber. In the middle ages, a celebrated monastery stood on its banks, at about 40 m. from Rome, NE of Cures. It is the ancient Farfarus.
FARFAR, a river of Transylvania, which falls into the Alauta, near Cronstadt.
FARFORTH, a parish of Lincolnshire, 6 m. S of Louth. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 92.
FARG, a small river in Perthshire, which rises in the Ochills, and, after a course of 101 m., falls into the Erne.
FARGAUM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Dowlatabad, 36 m. S of Ahmednuggur.
FARGEAU (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Yonne, on the Loing, 21 m. SW of Auxerre. Pop. of com., 2,348; of cant., 6,831.
FAR-HILL POINT, a headland near the mouth of Lyon inlet, in about N lat. 66° 24′, W long. 84° 39′.
FARIGLIANO, a small island near the E coast of Sicily, in E long. 15° 15', N lat. 37° 35'.
FARILHAO, a group of islets off the coast of Portugal, 12 m. NW of Cape Peniche.
FARIM, a small kingdom of W. Africa, with a cap. of the same name, situated on the river Cacheo or St. Domingo, 50 m. NW of Jeba.
FARINCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, cant. of Le-Fayl-Billot. Pop. 234.
FARINA (CAPE). See ABIAD (RAS-EL).
FARINDOLA, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo- Ultra, 18 m. SSE of Teramo. Pop. 2,556.
FARINGDON, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 2½ m. S of Alton. Area 2,430 acres. Pop. in 1801, 387; in 1841, 545.
FARJAN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Irak, 75 m. SW of Hamadan.
FARLAM, a parish in Cumberland, 3 m. ESE of Brampton, near the Carlisle and Newcastle railway. Area 5,680 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,035.

FARLEIGH (East), a parish in Kent, 23 m. SSW of Maidstone, on the S bank of the Medway. Area 2,050 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,461; in 1841. 1,377. On an average of 7 years to 1835, there had annually been 577 acres devoted to the cultivation of hops in this parish.

FARLEIGH (WEST), a parish in Kent, 33 m. SW of Maidstone, on the S bank of the Medway. Area 1,050 acres. Pop. in 1831, 392; in 1841, 403. Like East F., this parish is particularly noted for its excellent fruit, and its valuable hop-plantations. The average number of acres planted with hops for 7 years to 1835, was 183.

FARLEIGH-HUNGERFORD, a parish and v. in Somerset, <math>6\frac{1}{4}</math> m. SSE of Bath, on the river Frome, in a well-wooded country. Area of p. 840 acres. Pop. 154. The church stands on the summit of an acclivity, up the side of which the v. is built. The v. claims a very remote antiquity.
FARLEIGH-WALLOP, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 33 m. SSW of Basingstoke. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 94.
FARLETON, a township in the p. of Melling, co.-palatine of Lancaster, 81 m. NE of Lancaster. Area 690 acres. Pop. in 1831, 90.

FARLEY, a township in the p. of Alveton, co. of Stafford, 41 m. ENE of Cheadle. Pop. in 1801, 321; in 1841, 86.—Also a parish in Surrey, 5½ m. SE by S of Croydon. Area 960 acres. Pop. in 1801, 95; in 1841, 86.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Alderbury, Wilts, 5 m. E of Salisbury. Area 700 acres. Pop. in 1831, 254; in 1841, 298.
FARLEY-CHAMBERLAYNE, a parish in the co. of Southampton, <math>5\frac{3}{4}</math> m. WSW of Winchester. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. in 1801, including Flackstead, 148; in 1841, 149.

FARLINGTON, a parish in the co. of Southampton, <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> m. W of Havant. Area 2,070 acres. Pop. in 1801, 302; in 1841, 792.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Sheriff-Hutton, N. R. of Yorkshire, 51 m. ESE of Easingwold. Area 1,490 acres. Pop. in 1801, 174; in 1841, 181.
FARLOW, a chapelry in the p. of Stottesden, Salop, 10 m. NE by E of Ludlow. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. in 1801, 301; in 1841, 361.
FARLSTHORP, a parish in the co. of Lincoln, 2 m. SE of Alford. Area 1,140 acres. Pop. in 1801, 88; in 1841, 109.
FARMANBY, a township, partly in the p. of Ellerburn, partly in that of Thornton-Dale, N. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. ESE of Pickering. Area 2,530 acres. Pop. in 1801, 310; in 1841, 470.
FARMBOROUGH, a parish in Somerset, 7 m. WSW of Bath. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. in 1801, 532; in 1841, 1,149.
FARMINGTON, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 14 m. NE of Northleach. Area 2,470 acres. Pop. in 1801, 216; in 1841, 359.

FARMINGTON, a township of Franklin co., in the state of Maine, U.S., 32 m. NW of Augusta. Pop. in 1840, 2,613. The village is situated at the junction of Sandy and Little Norridgewock rivers.— Also a township of Strafford co., in the state of New Hampshire, 38 m. ENE of Concord. Pop. 1,380.— Also a township of Hartford co., in the state of Connecticut, 9 m. SW of Hartford, situated on the Farmington canal, which extends from New Haven to Northampton, in the state of Massachusetts, and intersected by two mountain ridges running NNE and SSW. It is watered by a river of the same name. The soil, except on the banks of the river, is light and sandy. Pop. 2,041. The village stands on an elevated plain, at the base of the E range, and consists chiefly of a single street, containing about 100 dwellings.-Also a township of Ontario co., in the state of New York, drained by Mad creek, an affluent of Canandaigua outlet, and intersected by the Auburn and Rochester railroad. It has a level surface, and its soil is clayey and difficult of cultivation, but generally productive. Limestone is abundant in all parts of the township. Pop. 2,122.—Also a township of Venango co., in the state of Pennsylvania. ~ FAR Pop. 799.—Also a township of Tioga co., in the same state. Pop. 503.—Also a township of Trumbull co., in the state of Ohio, on the head waters of Grand river. Pop. 1,172.—Also a township of Oakland co., in the state of Michigan, 20 m. NW of Detroit, watered by the N branch of Rouge river and Power's creek. Pop. 1,684.—Also a village of Van Buren co., pleasantly situated in a fine agricultural locality, and abounding in iron-ore, coal, and building-stone. -Also a village of Marshall co., in the state of Tennessee, 54 m. S of Nashville, on a branch of Duck river. Pop. 100.-Also a village of Fulton co., in the state of Illinois, 78 m. NW of Springfield.—Also a village of St. Francis co., in the state of Missouri, 139 m. SE of Jefferson city, on the head waters of Big river. Pop. 250.
FARMINGTON FALLS, a village of Farmington township, Franklin co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 27 m. NW of Augusta, on Sandy river.
FARMLUTEN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia.
FARMOUTIER, a town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. and 9 m. N of Rosoy. Pop. 1,200. It possesses an active trade in corn, and has an annual fair.
FARMSUM, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Groningen, about a mile SSE of Delfzyl. Pop. 915.
FARMVILLE, a village of Prince Edward co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., 70 m. WSW of Richmond, on the S side of Appomatox river. Pop. in 1840, 900.
FARNAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Saint-Chamond. Pop. 725. It has a fine steel manufactory.
FARNBACH, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, 8 m. WNW of Nuremberg, on a small river of the same name. Pop. 500. It has a castle, and a large brewery.

FARNBOROUGH, or FARMBOROUGH, a parish of Berkshire, 44 m. NW of East Ilsley. Area 2,720 acres. Pop. 204.—Also a parish in the co. of Hants, 6 m. NNE of Farnham, intersected by the Reading and Reigate railway. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. 356. -Also a parish in Kent, 44 m. SSE of Bromley. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 680.—Also a parish of Warwickshire, 63 m. SE of Kington, on the post road from Warwick to Banbury. Area 2,430 acres. Pop. 367.

FARNDALE (East-Side), a township in the p. of Lastingham, N. R. of Yorkshire, 11 m. NNW of Pickering. Area 4,850 acres. Pop. 383. FARNDALE, (HIGH-QUARTER OF WEST-SIDE), a township in the p. of Lastingham, N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. N of Kirkby-Moorside. Area 8,950 acres. Pop. 275.
FARNDALE (Low-Quarter), a township in the p. of Kirkby-Moorside, N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. N of Kirkby-Moorside. Area 3,560 acres. Pop. 188.
FARNDISH, a parish of Bedfordshire, 4 m. SSW of Higham-Ferrers. Area 810 acres, of which 300 are in Northamptonshire. Pop. 86.

FARNDON, a parish and township of Cheshire, 7½ m. SSE of Chester, on the Dee. Area of p. 3,050 acres. Pop. 999. Area of township 1,120 acres. Pop. 521.—Also a parish of Nottinghamshire, 2<sup>1</sup>/<sub>4</sub> m. WSW of Newark, on the E bank of the Trent. Area 1,710 acres. Pop. 575.
FARNDON (EAST), a parish of Northamptonshire, 2 m. SSW of Market-Harborough, in Leicestershire. Area 1,070 acres. Pop. 250.
FARNDON (WEST), a hamlet in the p. of Woodford, Northamptonshire. Pop. 128.
FARNEBO, a village of Sweden, in the province of Gefleborg, 10 m. SW of Hedsunda, and 30 m. SW of Gefle.

FARNESE, a town of the Pontifical states, in the delegation and 25 m. WNW of Viterbo. FARNE or FERN ISLANDS, two groups of little islands and rocks, 17 in number, in the p. of Holy island, co.-palatine of Durham, to the SE of Holy island or Lindisfarne. That which lies nearest to the shore,-House island,-is 1 m. 68 chains from the coast, and the most distant about 7 m. A lighthouse has been erected on this island, and another on Staples island, about 3 m. to the E of it. The passage between is open for vessels of any burden, but is dangerous from a cluster of rocks that lie in the middle of it. There is generally from 5 to 8 fath. of water in this road. Amongst the variety of birds which breed on the F. islands and rocks, are cormorants, eider-ducks, puffins, hawks, guillemots, shags, gulls of every description, kittiwakes, great terns, sea-pies, sea-larks, rock-larks, rock pigeons, and jackdaws, the last of which breed in rabbit holes.
FARNELL, a parish in Forfarshire, 4 m. SSE of Brechin. Pop. 620.

FARNHAM, a parish in Essex, 21 m. N of Bishop Stortford, and W of the Stort. Area 1,360 acres. Pop. 549.—Also a township in the p. of Allenton, Northumberland, 6 m. W of Rothbury, bounded on the W by the Cocket. Pop. 40.—Also a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. SW of Saxmundham. Area 740 acres. Pop. 186.—Also a parish and town in Surrey, 10 m. WSW of Guilford, and 38 m. WSW of London, N of the Wye. It includes the tythings of Badshot and Runfold, Culverlands and Tilford, Runwick, and Wrecklesham and Bourn. Area 10,510 acres. Pop. in 1801, 4,321; in 1831, 5,858; in 1841, 6,615. ing 700 ft. above sea-level, consists principally of one street, containing several good houses. The cloth manufacture was at one time carried on here to a considerable extent, but has totally declined. Hop-bags, painted canvass, or floor cloth, sheeting, huckabacks, sailcloth, and tarpawlings are woven here by handloom. A checked fabric, in use as carpeting, is likewise made from jute, or Indian hemp, of threads half the thickness of the little finger. The material is brought from Dundee, having there undergone the process of dyeing with vivid colours. The soil in this vicinity is peculiarly adapted to the cultivation of hops. On an average of 7 years ending in 1835, no less than 902 acres were annually appropriated to the cultivation of hops in this p.; being greatly above the quantity thus appropriated in any one p. in England during the same period. The average number of lbs. of hops charged with excise duty was 512,011, and the duty £4,266. The Farnham hops are highly celebrated, and preferred for the paleness of colour, and delicate flavour which they give to malt liquor; and on this account they are commonly sold at a price one-third greater than that paid for the hops of other districts. Wyehillfair is the great F. hop-mart. The Farnham market was once the largest for wheat in England, 400 loads having been sold in one day; but its importance has of late years much diminished; the produce of Hampshire, Sussex, &c., being now sent by watercarriage to London. Very plentiful springs of excellent water exist in the hill on which F. is built. Henry de Blois, bishop of Winchester, built, on the hill overlooking the town, a castle of great strength, which, after being demolished by Henry III., was rebuilt with more than its original magnificence, and possessed as the episcopal palace. It was laid in ruins by the parliamentary army; but after the restoration was rebuilt and repaired by Morley, bishop of Winchester, at an expense of £8,000, and continues to this day the principal palace belonging to that see. F. was the birth-place of the celebrated political writer, William Cobbett, in 1762. He died at Normandy-farm, near this town, in 1835; and his remains are interred near the main-entrance of the church.—Also a parish and township in the W. R. FAR of the Ure. Area of p. 2,780 acres. Pop. 580. Area of township 1,350 acres. Pop. 170.
FARNHAM (LITTLE), a parish of Dorsetshire, 7 m. WNW of Cranborne. Area 2,200 acres. Pop. 117.
FARNHAM-ROYAL, a parish of Buckinghamshire, 4 m. N of Windsor, near the Western railway. Area 2,910 acres. Pop. 1,258.
FARNHAM-TOLLARD, a tything in the p. of Tollard-Royal, 8 m. WNW of Cranborne. Pop. 224.
FARNHILL, a township in the p. of Kildwick, W. R. of Yorkshire, 31 m. SSE of Skipton, and intersected by the Leeds and Liverpool canal. Area 400 acres. Pop. 459.
FARNHURST, or FERNHURST, a parish in Sussex, 41 m. N of Midhurst. Area 4,680 acres. Pop. 762.
FARNINGHAM, a parish in Kent, 6 m. ESE of Foots-Cray. Area 2,880 acres. Pop. 701.

FARNLEY, a chapelry in the p. of St. Peter, within the parliamentary borough of Leeds, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. WSW of Leeds. Area 2,070 acres. Pop. 1,530.—Also a township in the p. and 2 m. NE of Otley, on the N bank of the Warfe. Area 1,810 acres. Pop. 217.
FARNLEY-TYAS, a township in the p. of Almondbury, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SSW of Huddersfield. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. 844.

FARNRODA, a village of the duchy of Saxe- The town, which is built on the S side of a hill, ris- Weimar, in the bail. and 5 m. SE of Eisenach. Pop. 671. It has manufactories of hosiery and of some woollen fabrics.
FARNSFIELD, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 4 m. WNW of Southwell. Area 3,920 acres. Pop. 1,099.

FARNWORTH, a chapelry in the p. of Deane, Lancashire, 3 m. SE of Great Bolton. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 4,829.—Also a parochial chapelry in the p. of Prescot, <math>5\frac{3}{4}</math> m. W of Warrington, N of the Mersey, and near the Liverpool and Manchester railway. Pop. 3,836.

FARO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, situated in a level and sandy plain, about a league from the sea, on the river Da Quarteira, or Valfermosa, in N lat. 37° 2'. Pop. 8,440. The town, which is quite open, is regularly built, and has tolerably broad streets composed of small houses. The houses in that part of the town round the church of San Pedro have still a Gothic appearance, as this was the only part of the town which escaped when F. was plundered and burnt by the English in 1596. There is a handsome square with some considerable buildings skirting the river; and one side of the town is defended by a small citadel. F. is the see of a bishop, suffragan of Evora. It contains 3 monasteries. Small vessels can come up to the town, but large ones are obliged to unload in the road, or lower down the river, which, after numerous windings, forms the narrow entrance of the harbour 11 league to the SE of the town. The harbour is defended by the fort of San Lourenço de Olhao, situated on the E side of the river. Another narrow arm of the river, or properly speaking of the sea, called a baretta, forms an island, on which is the sandy cape of Santa- Maria. The tract between the town and the sea is very marshy, and is covered with marine plants. It is flat and sandy on the opposite side; and at a distance rise the mountains of San-Miguel, which are high and steep, but well-cultivated towards their base. The greater part of the trade of Algarve is carried on at F. Wine and fruits, but particularly figs, are exported in great quantities. The figs are deposited by the country-people in a heap, in a building for this purpose. A syrup flows from them while in this stage which is used in making brandy. of Yorkshire, 2 m. N of Knaresborough, on a branch | They are then spread in the sun in an open situation, and are afterwards pressed into small baskets made of the leaves of the fan-palm, each containing 28 lbs. They are chiefly of the white kind, though the red, particularly the Figo-do-Enchario, and F.-do-Bispo, are most esteemed. Oranges and Spanish reeds are also exported from F. to England.-Also a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca and 32 m. N of Ourique, and 14 m. WNW of Beja, on the I. bank of the Safrins .- Also a small port of the island of Siphanto, on the SE coast, Grecian archipelago, in N lat. 36° 58'.

FARO, a town of Brazilian Guayana, in the prov. of Para, on Lake Jamunda, 45 m. NW of the confluence of the Rio Jamunda with the Amazon, and about 60 m. W of Obidos. Cacao and cotton are extensively cultivated in the environs. Butter manufactured from the eggs of the turtle, forms also an important object of local industry.

FARO (CAPO-DI), a low headland of Sicily, at the NE angle of the island, at the entrance of the strait of Messina, and 9 m. NE of the town of that name, in N lat. 38° 16', and E long. 15° 41'; 6,047 yds. from the rock of Scylla. At its extremity is the Torre-di-Faro, a fortress and lighthouse, commanding the narrowest part of the strait; and to the SW is a village of the same name. This promontory is the Pelorus or Pelorum Promontorium of the ancients. The adjacent v. is small and dirty; but the environs are picturesque, and still produces copiously the rough wine formerly so celebrated under | breed, and sheep, amounting to upwards of 35,000, the name of Mamertium.

FARO, or FARON (ISLAND), an island of Sweden, in the Baltic, ENE of the island of Gothland, from which it is separated by a narrow sound of the same name, in N lat. 57° 50', and E long. 19° 15'. It is about 10 m. in length from N to S, and 6 m. in average breadth. On its E side is a village of the same name.

FAROE, or Feroe Islands, a group lying to the NW of Denmark, between the parallels of 61° 20' and 62° 20' N; about 320 m. SE of Iceland. They are 22 in number, and 17 of them are inhabited; the rest being mere cliffs and holms. The principal island is Stromoe, which lies in the centre of the group, and is 27 m. long by about 7 m. broad. Oesteroe, to the E of Stromoe, is 20 m. long, and 10 m. broad. Sandoe, to the S of Stromoe, is 13 m. long, and 11 m. broad. Suderoe, to the S of Sandoe, is 17 m. in length, and 5 m. broad. The other inhabited islands are Punthaon, Vaagoe, Fugloe, Svinoe, Videroe, Bordoe, Konoe, Kalsoe, Kolter, Hestoe, Nolsoe, Skuoe, and Dimon. Their total superficial extent has been estimated at 500 sq. m.; and the inhabitants in 1812 were returned at 5,209; in 1840, 7,314; in 1847, 7,800; although they could support a larger pop. These islands are composed of trap and basaltic rocks. The most common characters of the trap are amygdaloidal and porphyritic. The Skälings-field in the island of Stromoe rises to a height of nearly 2,430 ft.; and the Slattaretind, in Oesteroe, has an alt. of 2,804 ft., above the level of the sea. In the latter island there occurs a range of basaltic pillars almost as regularly defined as those of Staffa.—The principal minerals are copper, jasper, and coals. The latter mineral might, it is reported, be worked to great advantage in Suderoe. A few lakes exist in some of the islands, and abundance of torrents and rivulets; but there is no opening between the mountains which can be called a valley, nor stream that can be dignified with the name of a river. On the coasts, the masses of rock in many places, viewed at a distance, suggest the idea of extensive walls, towers, castles, and spires, with an effect often strikingly picturesque and sometimes sublime. The shores are in many places so steep that the inhabitants can only III. * FAR reach the beach by the help of cords. The best havens are those of Westmann-haven on the W coast of Stromoe, and Kingshaven on the island of Oesteroe. But the tides in the straits between the different islands are extremely rapid, and frequently form dangerous maelstreams or whirpools, while frequent squalls increase the danger of the navigator.—The climate is rigorous, but not so much so as might have been expected in such a latitude. The winter is as mild as in Denmark. The high northern situation of these islands renders the days in summer very long, and in winter very short: the longest day of summer is 20 hours, and the shortest day of winter, 4 hours; but the clear twilight and frequent aurora borealis, render the latter less tedious to these islanders.—The soil is thin, mossy, and wet, offering little encouragement to the agriculturist, but occasionally affording good pasture. About a sixtieth part of the surface is cultivated. A little barley, however, is raised; and a few small hard turnips, and watery potatoes, are grown. The land is prepared for the seed by the spade,—the thin soil not admitting the employment of the plough; in some situations, they cover the flat rocky surfaces of stones with soil, upon which they raise potatoes and turnips. Trees are unknown; the only fruit is that of a few different species of wild berries. Hay produced from natural grass is the principal crop: what grain is needed is mostly imported from Denmark. Cows of a small form the principal wealth of the inhabitants. The latter are of a very peculiar breed, and remain constantly exposed to the weather. The horses, which are very diminutive in stature, are only used for bearing burdens, wheel-carriages being totally unknown.—This group was discovered and colonized by fugitive Normans, between the years 858 and 868. It does not appear to have attracted much notice from Britain at any period. During the American war the F. islands became notorious for smuggling, and continued to be so till the annihilation of the Dutch and Danish East India trade. The islanders are a laborious and simple race of men, leading a very primitive life, although by no means destitute of the necessaries of life. Their ordinary food is barley, milk, flesh, fish, and bread. Beer is considered a luxury, and spirits are only used on solemn occasions. Their language is a Danish dialect of the Norse.—The pop. of the F. islands is supported chiefly by bird-catching and fishing. Bird-catching —here a most arduous and hazardous employment— is prosecuted with great alacrity by the islanders, who ascend the most frightful cliffs with a long pole, having a bag-net fastened to the end, through the meshes of which the birds thrust their heads and hang suspended by the neck. The principal fishery is that of cod, though that species of fish is said to have become less abundant than formerly; herrings are also taken occasionally, and whales sometimes appear upon the coasts. The seal-fishery in the month of September is often very productive. Manufactures and trade, as may well be supposed, do not occupy much of the attention of these simple islanders. Every man, for the most part, practises the whole of the handicraft arts, and is his own weaver, tailor, tanner, shoemaker, mason, carpenter, boatbuilder, fisher, bird-catcher, and farmer. They contrive, however, to manufacture a few jackets, and upwards of 112,000 pairs of hose or coarse stockings annually, which, along with their surplus raw produce, consisting of train-oil, feathers, skins, and sometimes fish, tallow, and butter, they exchange with the Danes for grain and pulse, fishing-materials, timber, tar, nails, lead, tobacco, tin, coffee, salt, sugar, and other luxuries. - With regard to government, these islands are under nearly the same regulations as the other Danish colonies, especially Iceland. A military officer, with the command of 30 men, is first in authority, with the title of amtmann or bailiff; and under him are the treasurer and the governors of the syssals or districts, which are 6 in number, viz. Stromoe, Norderoe, Oesteroe, Waagde, Sandoe, and Suderoe. The expenses of the establishment are supplied by the royal domains, and by taxes imposed on the produce of the country, particularly on tallow, wool, and sheepskins.-The religion is Lutheran; and the direction of ecclesiastical affairs is vested in a provost, who acts under the bishop of Sieland. The islands are divided into 7 parishes; and in these 7 parishes there are 39 places of worship, so that almost every village has a church. -There are a great many villages and hamlets scattered over these islands, but there is nothing resembling a town, Thorshaven excepted, which stands on a small tongue of land on the SE side of the island of Stromoe, is the seat of government, and contains a pop. of about 720 souls. The houses are constructed of wood, closely crowded together, and roofed with birch-bark from Norway, over which is laid a layer of turf, which, from the dampness of the climate, retains at all times the same verdant appearance as the adjacent fields. The interior of the houses is generally neat, clean, and convenient. islands of Falster and Sieland, in N lat. 54° 57', and E long. 12° 2'.
FAR-OUT-HEAD, a cape of Sutherlandshire, E of Cape Wrath, in N lat. 58° 36', and W long. 4° 45'.

FARQUHAR'S ISLANDS, a group of six islands in the Indian ocean, NNE of Madagascar, and 750 m. NNW of Bourbon,—on the government of which they are dependent,—in N lat. 10° 7', and E long. 51° 13'. They lie in a horse-shoe form, comprising a circuit of about 9 m., opening to the N, and affording good anchorage. They produce cocoa-nuts in great abundance, and their coasts afford excellent fishing. They are chiefly of coral structure.
FARQUHAR'S ISLANDS (GOVERNOR), a group of islets, 9 in number, in the South Pacific, in S lat. 7° 40', and E long. 151° 30'. They are enclosed on all sides except the S by the Lahou shoal.
FARR. See FAR.
FARRA, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Souronga, 110 m. SW of Yedo, on the N bank of the bay of Tootomy.
FARRALINE (LOCH), a small sheet of water in Inverness-shire, 2½ m. E of Loch Ness.
FARRANI, or FARANI, a town of Sudan, in the kingdom of Ludamar, 36 m. NW of Benum.

FARRAR ISLANDS, a group of low sandy islands in the Arabian gulf, 53 m. from the coast of Yemen, 20 m. NW of Kumfidah. Their S extremity is in N lat. 19° 17'; and they extend 13 m. in a NNW direction.
FARRER, an important branch of the river Beauly, Inverness-shire, which rises in Loch Monar, and flows E through Glen Farrer till it joins the Glass.
FARRERA, a village of Switzerland, cant. of the Grisons, in a wild valley N of Madris. Pop. 400.

FARRI, a town of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Efat, 18 m. NE of Ankobar, 1 m. W of Dinnomali. It stands upon an open depressed space on the summit of a hill, between four or five elevations, into which the summit is divided, each of which is surmounted by a little group of houses, while the hollow in the midst forms a kind of green or market-place. Many of the houses are partially excavated in the soft trachytic stone of which the hill is composed. The surrounding rocks and soil abound with the nitrate of potass.—Johnson. FAR
FARRINGDON, a chapelry in the p. of Iwerne- Courtnay or Shrowton, Dorsetshire, 51 m. SSW of Shaftesbury. Pop. 34.—Also a parish in the co. of Hants. Area 2,430 acres. Pop. 545.

FARRINGDON (GREAT), a market - town and parish of Berkshire, 36 m. WNW of Reading, and 69 m. WNW of London, and about 2 m. S of the Thames, and 6 m. N of Farringdon-road station of the Great Western railway. Area of p. 6,910 acres. Pop. 3,593. The town is finely situated at the foot of Farringdon-hill, a beautiful eminence rising from the vale of Whitehorse, and commanding an extensive view over parts of Oxfordshire, Gloucestershire, and Wiltshire, in addition to the whole of the beautiful vale beneath. The town is one of the pollingplaces for the members for Berkshire. It has few manufactures; the chief trade is in hogs and bacon. The navigation of the Thames, which approaches within 2 m. of the town, affords a ready conveyance for commodities connected with the trade of this place; and the Great Western railway, passing to the S of F., was opened from London to the F.-road station on 20th July 1840. A large and numerously attended corn-market is held weekly on Tuesday.
FARRINGDON (LITTLE), a tything in the p. of Langford, Berkshire, 2 m. NE of Lechlade. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 153.
FARRINGTON, a township in the p. of Penwor-
FAROE, an island of Denmark, between the tham, Lancashire, 4 m. S of Preston, intersected by the Wigan and Preston railway. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 1,719.
FARRINGTON-GURNEY, a parish in Somerset, 8½ m. NNE of Wells. Area 890 acres. Pop. 605.
FAR-ROCKAWAY, a village of Hempstead township, Queen's co., in the state of New York, U. S., on a peninsula between Jamaica bay and the Atlantic. Pop. 120.
FARROILIP, a group of islets, 3 in number, in the N. Pacific, in the Caroline archipelago, in N lat. 10° 8', E long. 46° 45'.
FARRUCH (CAPE), a headland of the island of Majorca, on the E coast, in N lat. 39° 47', E long. 3° 23'.

FARS, or Farsistan, a province of Persia, bounded on the N by Irak and Khorasan; on the E by Kirman and Luristan; on the W by Khuzistan; and on the S by the Persian gulf. It lies between the parallels of 27° 40' and 31° 52' N; and has an area of about 50,000 sq. m. It is divided into two regions,-the warm and the cold,—which are respectively designated by the names of Gurmsir and Sirhud. The warm climate extends from the sea to the lat. of Kazerun, and then runs parallel with the gulf, from the banks of the Tab to the confines of Luristan. When the periodical rains are abundant, the sandy plain at the foot of the mountain-ranges which separate the tableland of Iran from the Persian gulf, produces a considerable quantity of dry grain; but when the rains are less abundant, a famine generally ensues. This sandy plain, which is called Dushtistan or Gurmsir, -the latter word being the generic term for a hot region,—is divided into the districts of Liwari and Hiaduat, which are separated from each other by a mountain-range called Kopi Bung. Both these districts, which contain only a few wretched mud villages, are badly cultivated. The Sirhud or cold region stretches from the parallel of Kazerun to that of Yezdikhast, on the borders of Irak. It comprehends most of the mountainous part of Fars. The mountain-valleys in this quarter are generally 8 or 10 m. broad, and from 15 to 100 m. long; they afford abundance of pasturage, and are commonly fertile, though ill-cultivated. The plains of Shiraz, Kazerun, and Merdesht, however, are in good cultivation; but towards the N and W are almost unpeopled. Kinneir travelled in 1809 above 60 m. between Behaban and Shiraz, through charming wooded valleys, without seeing the face of a human being. An ancient tribe which formerly inhabited them had been almost extirpated by orders of the prince; and the few which had escaped to the lofty mountain-ridges subsisted upon a wretched kind of bread made from acorns, and upon the pillage of travellers. The E part of the prov. towards Darabgherd and Fesa is more open. The soil is more sandy, and the plains are extensive. On ascending from the Dushtistan, by the pass of the Dochter and Pirazun, to the level of Shiraz, the trees that attract our notice are the chinar, the cypress, the pinaster, the Lombardy poplar, and the willow.—The range of mountains seen from the coast is not a separate range, but a branch of Mount Zagros, which stretches in a continued succession of ridges from the borders of the Persian gulf to the Caspian sea and Mount Caucasus. The hills towards Bushire are about 24 m. from the sea. The plain becomes contracted towards Bunder-Reight; and to the W of the village of Gunava a low ridge suddenly projects to the S, and terminates at the sea-shore in the projecting point of Kohi-Bung, which separates the districts of Lirawi and Hiaduat. This point is about 7 or 8 m. broad, and beyond it lies the plain of Lirawi. The mountains are here again 20 m. distant from the sea; and for 18 or 19 m. they preserve this distance, but afterwards approach the S, and assume a circular form near Bunder-Dilum. On turning the S point of this low and advanced branch-which is called Zeitun, from a small town near Behaban—they again retire to the N, and at the port of the Mashur they are 30 m. from the sea. Their most southern extremity at Shuster crosses the 32d parallel N under the 49th meridian of E long.—The principal rivers are the Tab,—the Arosis of the ancients,—the Bund-Emir, and several others whose modern names are not mentioned. The Tab, which is the largest, is formed by the union of two streams near Zeitun, one of which rises at the foot of the high hill of Kamarah, and the other near that of Ardicune, about 45 m. NW of Shiraz. Kinneir considers this latter branch as the river mentioned by Arrian in the march of Alexander. The Tab in the lower part of its course separates Fars from Khuzistan; passes through the town of Indian, where it was 80 yds. wide in Feb., and navigable for boats of 20 tons burthen; and discharges itself into the sea about 16 m. below Indian. The water of this river is perfectly sweet when it passes Zeitun; but in running over the hills towards Indian, it becomes so brackish as hardly to be fit for use. This is also the case with all the other rivers in Fars which empty themselves into the Persian gulf. The river Bund-Emir, Bendamir, or Kur, called by some of the ancients Cyrus, and by the Greek historians Araxes, flows through the delightful valley of Merdesht; and in the rich districts of Kurjan, is subdivided into numerous streams for irrigating the fields. The remaining part of the river is 409. joined by Shamier, a small stream which rises in a hot fountain, 3 m. from Gazian, a town about 90 m. from Shiraz, and afterwards passes the villages of Kumu and Syvund. The united streams then discharge themselves into the lake of Bakhtigan.-In travelling from Bushire to Indian, Kinneir passed other four rivers. The first of these rises among the mountains behind the old city of Shapur; and after running through that city, and watering the villages of Kesht and Dalki, enters the gulf a little to the S of Rohilla. Kinneir supposes it to be the ancient Heratenis. At Rohilla it is 60 yds. broad, and 6 ft. deep.—The second river, which he supposes to be the Granis of Arrian, is only 7 m. to the NW of this. It discharges itself into the sea half-way between FAS Rohilla and Bunder-Reight; but it is neither so wide nor so deep as the former.—The third, which is the Rhoganis of Arrian, runs with a SW course from the mountains, and throws itself into the sea 3 m. NE of Gonass. It is almost equal in breadth to that of Rohilla; at high water it is impassable, but it is only 3 ft. deep during the ebb.—The last and the smallest river, which is probably the Brizana of Arrian, flows between Hissar and Bunder-Dilum. After a wandering course from the hills of Zeitun, it discharges itself into the gulf 8 m. SE of Bunder-Dilum.—The rivers in the S part of the prov. are very imperfectly known. The salt lake of Bakhtigan is about 75 m. in circumf., and is situated about 10 m. SE of Shiraz. In summer, when it is nearly dry, the people on its banks collect the salt which encrusts the bot- This salt is generally used throughout the tom. prov., and is reckoned very fine.—The principal productions of this prov. are corn, rice, dates, raisins, opium, saffron, cotton, hemp, silk, and wine. Many cattle and sheep are reared; and woollen, silk, and cotton stuffs are manufactured.—The principal towns in Fars are Shiraz, the capital; Khazerun, about 70 m. W of Shiraz; Shapur, Bushire, Bunder-Reight, a seaport with about 300 or 400 inhabitants; Bunder- Dilum, another seaport having about 700 inhabitants; Zeitun, with a pop. of about 2,000; Behaban, the capital of the mountainous district of Khogiloea; Fallayum, Selbistan, Niris, Feza, Darabgherd, and Ursinjan.—Kinneir.—De Bode.—Ainsworth.

FARSAN ISLANDS, a group in the Arabian gulf, 30 m. from the coast of Yemen, in N lat. 16° 50'. The largest is about 15 m. in length, and has a small harbour. It is populous, and abounds with gazelles.
FARSHOUT, a town of Upper Egypt, in the prov. and 22 m. ESE of Girgeh, on an inosculation of the Nile. The pasha has erected a cotton-spinning mill here.
FARSID, a village in the p. of Aghada, co. Cork. Area 18 acres. Pop. 212.
FARSLEY. See CALVERLEY WITH FARSLEY.
FARS-MODANLU, a nomade people of Persia, in the prov. of Farsistan, consisting of about 10,000 individuals, who speak the Turkish language.
FARSUND, a town of Norway, in the stift and 56 m. W of Christiansand, amt of Mandal, on the N. sea. Pop. 1,032. It has a port, and carries on an active trade, chiefly in dried fish.
FARTAGH, or FERTAGH, a parish of co. Kilkenny, 21 m. NNE of Urlingford. Area about 6,719 acres. Pop. 2,998.

FARTAK, or FURTUK (RAS), a cape of Arabia, in the prov. of Mahrah, in N lat. 15° 40', and E long. 52° 10′. To the WSW is the maritime village of Fartagh or Kersah.—Also a cape forming the E point of the gulf of Akabah, in N lat. 28° 6'.
FARTHINGHOE, a parish of Northamptonshire, 4 m. WNW of Brackley. Area 3,320 acres. Pop.
FARTHINGSTONE, a parish of Northamptonshire, 7 m. NW of Towcester. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 315.

FARVAGNY, a bailiwick, parish, and village of Switzerland, in the eant. and 8 m. SSW of Friburg, near the r. bank of the Sarine. Pop. 433. It has a castle and a chapel noted as a pilgrims' resort. Straw-work forms the chief branch of local industry.
FAR-WEST, a village of Caldwell co., in the state of Missouri, 169 m. NW of Jefferson city, 1 m. S of Shoal creek, and 30 m. N of the Missouri. Pop. in 1840, 500.
FASAD, a town of Afghanistan, in the prov. of Seistan, 40 m. SW of Jellalabad.

FASANA, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Istria, gov. and 69 m. S of Trieste, and circle of Pisano, on the Adriatic, on which it has a small port. Pop. 500, chiefly engaged in fishing. Near this town are the islands of Brioni, celebrated for their fine marbles.
FASANO, or FASCIANO, a town of Naples, in the Terra-d-Bari, district and 37 m. SE of Bari. Pop. 8,890. It contains 3 churches and a convent.
FASIKANI, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Mutsu, 128 m. N of Gido.
FASOVA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Volhynia, 50 m. E of Novrad-Volynsk.
FATA, a maritime town of Japan, in the island of Silkokf, prov. and 21 m. S of Tosa. Benjowski effected a landing here for a short time.

FATARELLA, a walled town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 38 m. WNW of Tarragona, partido of Gandesa. Pop. 1,495. It has a parish church, an hospital, and several convents, and possesses some cotton manufactures.
FATATENDA, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Wulli, on the r. bank of the Gambia, 20 m. SW of Medina. An English factory was established here, but was abandoned in 1734.

FATCHIOU, an island of Japan, in the group of the Tsus-sima islands, strait of Corea, in N lat. 34° 15', and E long. 129° 28. It is mountainous but fertile; 15 m. from N to S, and nearly 10 m. in average breadth.
FATEEREH, a village in the mountainous district of Upper Egypt, 55 m. NE of Gheneh. It stands in the midst of extensive ruins.
FATEJ, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 35 m. NW of Koursk, district and 32 m. E of Dmitriev, on a height near the r. bank of the Ousoja. Pop. 1,900.
FATIANAVO, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Pskov, 35 m. ESE of Ostrov.
FATIEH, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. of Bassora, 15 m. W of Korna, and 197 m. SSE of Bagdad, on the l. bank of the Euphrates, 15 m. above the junction of the Tigris.

FATIGAR, a prov. of Abyssinia, in the country of the Galla, ENE of the district of Shoa and Efat. It is watered by the Hawash. FATOWIVA, FATTUHU, OTTAHEVAH, or MAGDALENA ISLAND, an island in the South Pacific, in the group of the Marquesas islands or Mendana archipelago, in S lat. 10° 30′, and W long. 138° 25′. It is represented by Mendana, who, however, was unable to effect a landing upon it, to have steep banks, and to rise to a considerable elevation. It appeared populous, and had a harbour on the S side.

FATRA (NAGY), or GREAT FATRA, a range of mountains in Hungary, a branch of the Carpathian chain, extends in a N direction, along the confines of the comitats of Thurocz and Leptau, from the sources of the Revucza, to the banks of the Vag, a distance of about 21 m. Its highest summit, the Klatberg, rises to the height of 4,418 ft. above sealevel.
FATRA (KIS), or LITTLE FATRA, a mountain of Hungary, in the comitat of Arva, to the S of the Nagy-Fatra, from which it is separated by the Vag, and 9 m. W of Kubin.
